task: Screen the risk of building AI workflow tooling for healthcare reimbursement using de-identified work denials data and prior-auth case data.
summary: Healthcare reimbursement denial and prior-authorization case data remains highly linkable even after removal of direct identifiers, and may also be subject to employer, contractual, and healthcare privacy controls.
key_reasons: Denials and prior-auth case data likely contains protected or regulated healthcare information.
Quasi-identifiers such as dates, provider details, payer information, diagnosis/procedure combinations, and locations can enable linkage.
Unstructured notes, narratives, and attachments may retain residual identifiers.
Use of work data may violate employer policy, contracts, or regulated data handling requirements.
External AI tooling introduces vendor, retention, and model-training disclosure risk if not explicitly approved.
recommended_controls: Obtain written employer or data-owner approval.
Require privacy, compliance, and security review before use.
Prefer synthetic data or a formally approved limited dataset.
Keep any approved real-data workflow inside enterprise-controlled infrastructure.
Strip or generalize dates, geography, provider and payer identifiers, free text, and rare combinations.
Document de-identification and assess re-identification risk.
